concepts.title: Whole Numbers and Integers
concepts.definition: Whole numbers are {0, 1, 2, 3, ...}. Integers extend whole numbers to include negative numbers, i.e., {..., -3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3, ...}.
examples: −5, 0 and 12 are integers; 2.5 is not an integer.
Whole numbers are a subset of integers.

concepts.title: Representation on Number Line
concepts.definition: Integers can be shown on a horizontal line with zero at the centre, positive numbers to the right and negative numbers to the left, equally spaced.
examples: On the number line −3 is to the left of 2, so −3 < 2.

concepts.title: Addition and Subtraction of Integers
concepts.definition: Adding and subtracting integers follows rules based on their signs. Same signs add magnitudes and keep the sign; different signs subtract magnitudes and take the sign of the larger magnitude.
examples: 5 + (−3) = 2, because 5 − 3 = 2 and 5 has larger magnitude.

concepts.title: Multiplication of Integers
concepts.definition: Multiplication of integers follows sign rules: product of two integers with same sign is positive, with different signs is negative.

concepts.title: Division of Integers
concepts.definition: Division of integers uses the same sign rules as multiplication: quotient of two integers with same sign is positive, with different signs is negative.

concepts.title: Properties of Operations on Integers
concepts.definition: Integers are closed under addition, subtraction and multiplication and satisfy commutative, associative and distributive properties for these operations.
formulae: a + b = b + a (commutative for addition)
a × b = b × a (commutative for multiplication)
(a + b) + c = a + (b + c) (associative for addition)
(a × b) × c = a × (b × c) (associative for multiplication)
a × (b + c) = a × b + a × c (distributive)

concepts.title: The Token Model and Carrom Coin Analogy
concepts.definition: Visualising integer addition and subtraction using positive and negative counters or carrom coins (black for -1, white for +1).
examples: A black coin and a white coin together 'cancel' each other out to zero.
Subtractions like 2 - (-3) can be seen as 'adding' 3 more of the opposite color.

concepts.title: Historical Context: Brahmagupta's Rules (628 CE)
concepts.definition: Ancient rules for arithmetic on positive numbers (Dhana), negative numbers (Rina), and zero (Sunya).
examples: 'The product of two debts is a fortune' (Rina × Rina = Dhana).
'Zero multiplied by a debt or a fortune is zero' (Sunya × a = Sunya).

concepts.title: Types of Fractions
concepts.definition: Fractions can be proper (numerator < denominator), improper (numerator ≥ denominator) or mixed (a whole number plus a proper fraction).
examples: 3/4 is a proper fraction, 7/3 is improper, 2 1/5 is a mixed fraction.

concepts.title: Equivalent Fractions and Simplification
concepts.definition: Equivalent fractions represent the same value; they can be obtained by multiplying or dividing numerator and denominator by the same non‑zero number.
examples: 6/8 simplified by dividing numerator and denominator by 2 gives 3/4.

concepts.title: Operations on Fractions
concepts.definition: Fractions are added or subtracted using a common denominator; multiplication multiplies numerators and denominators; division multiplies by the reciprocal.

concepts.title: Decimals and Place Value
concepts.definition: Decimals have a whole number part and a fractional part separated by a decimal point, with place values tenths, hundredths, thousandths and so on.
examples: In 4.327, 3 is tenths, 2 is hundredths and 7 is thousandths.

concepts.title: Operations on Decimals
concepts.definition: Addition and subtraction of decimals align the decimal points; multiplication and division follow whole‑number rules while managing decimal places.

concepts.title: Conversion Between Fractions and Decimals
concepts.definition: Fractions with denominators like 10, 100, 1000 can be written as decimals, and terminating decimals can be written as fractions.

concepts.title: Apavartana and Simplification
concepts.definition: The process of 'Apavartana' involves removing common factors from the numerator and denominator to find the simplest form.
examples: Umasvati (150 CE) used the term Apavartana for equivalent fraction reduction.
Simplifying 60/90 to 2/3 by cancelling 30.

concepts.title: Area Models and Fraction Multiplication
concepts.definition: Multiplication of fractions can be visualised as finding the area of a rectangle with fractional side lengths or using a Unit Square model.
formulae: Area = Length × Breadth (where both can be fractions)
examples: Multiplying 1/2 by 1/3 is equivalent to taking 1/3 of a 1/2 strip of a unit square.
Bhāskara I's geometric interpretation of product magnitude.

concepts.title: Bhāskara II's (Līlāvatī) Division Principle
concepts.definition: Division of fractions is the same as multiplication by the reciprocal of the divisor.
examples: How many 1/4 portions are there in 2? (2 ÷ 1/4 = 8).
The miser and the beggar: Sharing drammas and cowrie shells.

concepts.title: Precision and the Decimal Point
concepts.definition: The decimal point represents increasing precision. Historical systems like al-Uqlīdīsī's used different symbols before the modern dot.
examples: Leap Year precision: 365.2422 days vs simple 365.25.
Real-world errors: Air Canada Flight 143 fuel calculation mistake (Metric vs Imperial).

concepts.title: Cyclic Numbers and Special Patterns
concepts.definition: Some fractions produce repeating decimal patterns with unique properties, like Cyclic Numbers.
examples: Cyclic Number 142857 from 1/7: 142857 × 2 = 285714 (same digits rotated).
Irukandji jellyfish: Measuring tiny organisms using decimals (2.5 cm).

concepts.title: Data and Frequency
concepts.definition: Data are collected facts or numbers; frequency of a value is the number of times it occurs in the data.
examples: If the marks 5, 7, 5, 8, 5 occur, the frequency of 5 is 3.

concepts.title: Tally Marks and Tables
concepts.definition: Tally marks are quick strokes used to count frequencies; grouped into fives for easy reading and organised in tables.
examples: |||| represents 4 and ||||/ represents 5 in tally marks.

concepts.title: Bar Graphs
concepts.definition: Bar graphs represent data using rectangular bars with heights proportional to frequencies on a set of axes.
examples: A bar graph can show the number of students choosing different sports.

concepts.title: Mean, Median and Mode
concepts.definition: Mean is the average of data, median is the middle value when data are arranged in order, and mode is the most frequent value.
formulae: Mean = (sum of all observations) / (number of observations)
examples: For data 2, 4, 6: mean = (2 + 4 + 6) / 3 = 4; median = 4; any value occurring most often is the mode.

concepts.title: Chance and Probability (Informal)
concepts.definition: Chance describes how likely an event is to happen, using terms like certain, likely, unlikely and impossible.
examples: Getting a number greater than 6 on a standard die is impossible.
Getting a head when tossing a coin is equally likely as getting a tail.

concepts.title: Statistical Thinking: Questions and Statements
concepts.definition: Statistics involves collecting, organising, and analysing varied data. A statistical question expects a range of answers (e.g., 'How tall are students?'), while a statistical statement is a numerical summary or prediction.
examples: Question: 'What was the rainfall pattern in Barmer last year?'
Statement: 'I take about 15 minutes to cycle from home.'

concepts.title: Arithmetic Mean: Fair-Share and Indian Terminology
concepts.definition: The Mean is the 'equalising' value found by dividing the sum of values by their count. Ancient Indian mathematicians called it 'samamiti' (mean measure) or 'samikarana' (equalising).
formulae: Mean = (Sum of all values) / (Number of values)
examples: Fair-share: 30 guavas shared by 5 friends = 6 each.
Historical terms: Samarajju (Brahmagupta, 628 CE), Samikarana (Mahāvīrāchārya, 850 CE), and Sāmya (Śrīpati, 1039 CE).
Jacob Kobel's Rod (16th century): Mean foot size of 16 adult males.

concepts.title: Dot Plots, Variability and Range
concepts.definition: A dot plot visualises patterns and clusters. Variability describes the spread, while 'Range' is the difference between the maximum and minimum values.
examples: Onion prices in Yahapur (clustered) vs Wahapur (spread/variable).
Range in Wahapur: ₹60 - ₹17 = ₹43.

concepts.title: Outliers and Robustness of Median
concepts.definition: The Median is the middle value of sorted data. It is robust against outliers—extreme values that skew the Arithmetic Mean.
examples: Poovizhi's family heights: Median (170 cm) represents the group better than Mean when a child (outlier) is present.
Zero Median: Case where a cricket team scores high despite most players scoring 0.

concepts.title: Central Tendency and Distribution Patterns
concepts.definition: Mean and Median are measures of where data 'piles up'. Their relative positions indicate distribution balance (Mean < Median or Mean > Median).
examples: Mean < Median: Influenced by lower-side values.
Mean > Median: Influenced by higher-side outliers.

concepts.title: Clustered Column Graphs
concepts.definition: Also called Double Bar Graphs, these compare multiple categories across different groups or periods side-by-side.
examples: Comparing monthly onion prices in Yahapur vs Wahapur.
Rocket launch comparison (2021-2023): SpaceX, ISRO, NASA (Chandrayaan-3, Sputnik-1 missions).

concepts.title: Comparative Inference and Seasonal Patterns
concepts.definition: Analysing graphs reveals geographic and seasonal facts, such as daylight patterns indicating hemispheric locations.
examples: Helsinki (Finland) vs Wellington (New Zealand) daylight patterns (Summer vs Winter inversion).
Midnight Sun in Andøya, Norway.

concepts.title: Variables and Constants
concepts.definition: A variable is a symbol (like x, y) that can take different values; a constant is a fixed number.
examples: In 3x + 5, x is a variable and 3, 5 are constants (3 also a coefficient).

concepts.title: Algebraic Expressions
concepts.definition: An algebraic expression is formed by combining variables and constants using operations like addition, subtraction, multiplication and division.
examples: 4x + 7, 3y − 2 and 2a + 3b + 5 are algebraic expressions.

concepts.title: Equations and Solutions
concepts.definition: An equation states that two expressions are equal and usually contains a variable; the solution is the value of the variable that makes the equation true.
examples: x + 5 = 9 is an equation; x = 4 is its solution.

concepts.title: Solving One‑Step Linear Equations
concepts.definition: Simple equations of type ax + b = c are solved by doing the same operation on both sides to isolate the variable.

concepts.title: Applications of Simple Equations
concepts.definition: Word problems can be translated into equations in one variable and solved using the same methods.
examples: If a number increased by 9 gives 20, let the number be x: x + 9 = 20 ⇒ x = 11.

concepts.title: The Weighing Scale Analogy
concepts.definition: An equation behaves like a balanced weighing scale. Equality is preserved if we add, subtract, multiply, or divide the same weight (number) on both sides (LHS and RHS).
examples: Two fried eggs balancing 6 units: 2e = 6.
Adding or removing equal weights from both plates keeps the scale balanced.

concepts.title: Inverse Operations and Transposition
concepts.definition: Equations are solved using inverse operations: Addition ↔ Subtraction and Multiplication ↔ Division. Transposing a term involves its additive inverse or reciprocal.
formulae: a + x = b → x = b - a
examples: 11y + (-5) = 61 becomes 11y = 66 by transposing (-5).
u/15 = 6 becomes u = 6 × 15.

concepts.title: Historical Context: Bījagaṇita and Notations
concepts.definition: Algebra was known as Bījagaṇita in ancient India (Bīja = seed). Brahmagupta (628 CE) and Aryabhata (499 CE) developed systematic methods. Fields Medalist David Mumford remarked that Brahmagupta is the 'key person' in the creation of algebra. Al-Khwarizmi (825 CE) later coined 'Algebra' (al-jabr).
formulae: x = (D - B) / (A - C) (Brahmagupta's formula for Ax + B = Cx + D)
examples: Ancient symbols: 'y' for yāvat-tāvat (unknown), 'k' for klaka (black), 'ni' for nilaka (blue), and 'r' for rūpa (known constants).
Bakhshali Manuscript (300 CE): Problem of four persons where the second gets twice the first, third thrice the second, and fourth four times the third, totaling 132.
Bhāskarāchārya: Parity problem between 300 rupees + 6 horses and 10 horses + 100 rupees debt.

concepts.title: Algebraic Modelling and Patterns
concepts.definition: Real-life patterns and situations can be modelled into algebraic equations using variables and constants.
examples: Jasmine's Matchsticks: 2n + 1 = 99 stick positions.
Madhubanti's Party: 25p + 50 = 500 (₹50 delivery + ₹25 per plate).
Riyaz's Math Trick: [(x - 3) × 4] + 8 = 24.

concepts.title: Complex Parity and Comparisons
concepts.definition: Equations can be used to find when two different growing quantities become equal.
examples: Jahnavi and Sunita's savings comparison: 4000 + 650m = 5050 + 500m.
Donkeys & Children riddle: 28 heads and 80 feet parity.

chapters.title: Lines and Angles (Parallel and Intersecting Lines)

concepts.title: Basic Terms: Point, Line, Ray, Line Segment
concepts.definition: A point shows a position; a line extends endlessly in both directions; a ray has one endpoint and extends endlessly in one direction; a line segment has two endpoints.
examples: A pencil tip represents a point, an edge of a ruler can model a line segment.

concepts.title: Types of Angles
concepts.definition: Angles are classified as acute (0°–90°), right (90°), obtuse (90°–180°), straight (180°), reflex (180°–360°) and complete (360°).
examples: A corner of a square is a right angle; a slice less than a quarter circle is an acute angle.

concepts.title: Pairs of Angles
concepts.definition: Adjacent, linear pair, vertically opposite, complementary and supplementary angles describe relationships between two angles.
formulae: Angles in a linear pair sum to 180°
examples: If two angles form a straight line, they are supplementary.
Opposite angles where two lines intersect are vertically opposite and equal.

concepts.title: Intersecting and Parallel Lines
concepts.definition: Intersecting lines meet at a point; parallel lines never meet, however far extended.
examples: Railway tracks are approximately parallel lines.

concepts.title: Proof of Equality: Vertically Opposite Angles
concepts.definition: Whenever two lines intersect, vertically opposite angles are always equal. This property is arrived at through logical reasoning (Linear pair property) rather than just measurement.
examples: In Fig 5.2, if ∠a is 120, then ∠b is 60, ∠c is 120 and ∠d is 60.
Measurements may show 179 or 181 due to thickness of lines or tool errors, but ideal geometry reasoning stays exact.

concepts.title: Parallelism in Paper Folding Patterns
concepts.definition: Folding paper can generate sets of parallel and perpendicular lines. Successive horizontal folds follow a specific mathematical pattern of growth.
formulae: P = 2^n + 1 (where n is the number of folds and P is the number of parallel creases)
examples: One horizontal fold creates 3 parallel lines; two folds create 5; three folds create 9.
Folding diagonally creates lines parallel to the diagonal.
Opposite edges of a square sheet are parallel; adjacent edges are perpendicular $(\angle = 90^\circ)$.

concepts.title: Transversals and Angle Systems
concepts.definition: A transversal is a line that intersects two or more lines at distinct points. It forms 8 angles, resulting in a maximum of four distinct angle measures.
examples: If a transversal crosses two lines, pairs of vertically opposite angles (like \angle 1 and \angle 3) remain equal.
Is it possible for all 8 angles to be different? No, because vertical opposite pairs must match.

concepts.title: Corresponding and Alternate Angles Axioms
concepts.definition: When a transversal intersects parallel lines, corresponding angles and alternate angles are equal. This equality is a necessary and sufficient condition for parallelism.
formulae: If \angle corresponding1 = \angle corresponding2, then L1 || L2
examples: Verification using tracing paper: Tracing \angle a and aligning it with \angle b to prove equality.
In Fig. 5.26, if ∠6 is 135, its corresponding angle ∠2 and alternate angle ∠4 are also 135.
Alternate internal angles (like ∠d and ∠f) form a Z-pattern.

concepts.title: Interior Angles on the Same Side (Co-interior)
concepts.definition: The sum of the interior angles on the same side of a transversal intersecting a pair of parallel lines is always 180 degrees.
examples: In Fig. 5.28, if AB || CD and ∠3 is 50, then ∠6 is 130.
In Fig. 5.29, if AB || CD and AD || BC, then ∠ADC + ∠DAB = 180. If ∠DAC = 65, then ∠CAB = 55.
In Fig. 5.34, if EA ⊥ AB and AB || EF, with ∠BEF = 55, then x = 125.

concepts.title: Geometrical Construction of Parallel Lines
concepts.definition: Parallel lines can be accurately drawn using geometry tools like a ruler and a set-square or through specific paper folding steps.
examples: Set-Square Method: Sliding the set-square along a fixed ruler to point A to ensure the same angle (90 or other) is maintained.
Paper Folding Method: Folding line 't' ⊥ 'l', then line 'm' ⊥ 't' ensures 'm' || 'l'.

concepts.title: Parallel Illusions
concepts.definition: Visual illusions where strictly parallel lines appear tilted or bent due to surrounding patterns and contrasting backgrounds.
examples: The 'Parallel Illusion' in Fig. 5.35 where the eye perceives lines as non-parallel despite geometrical proof.

chapters.title: The Triangle and Its Properties (A Tale of Three Intersecting Lines)

concepts.title: Classification of Triangles
concepts.definition: Triangles can be classified by sides as scalene, isosceles or equilateral and by angles as acute, right or obtuse.
examples: An equilateral triangle has all sides equal and all angles 60°.
A right triangle has one 90° angle.

concepts.title: Angle Sum Property
concepts.definition: The sum of the three interior angles of a triangle is always 180 degrees.
examples: If two angles are 50° and 60°, the third is 180° − (50° + 60°) = 70°.

concepts.title: Exterior Angle Property
concepts.definition: An exterior angle of a triangle is equal to the sum of the two interior opposite angles.
formulae: Exterior angle = sum of two interior opposite angles
examples: If an exterior angle is 120° and one opposite interior angle is 50°, the other is 70°.

concepts.title: Triangle Inequality
concepts.definition: In a triangle, the sum of any two sides is greater than the third side.
examples: Sides 2 cm, 3 cm, 6 cm cannot form a triangle because 2 + 3 is not greater than 6.

concepts.title: Medians and Altitudes
concepts.definition: A median joins a vertex of a triangle to the midpoint of the opposite side; an altitude is a perpendicular from a vertex to the opposite side or its extension.
examples: Every triangle has three medians and three altitudes.

concepts.title: Efficient Geometrical Construction (SSS)
concepts.definition: Constructing triangles by given sidelengths is most efficient using a compass to draw intersecting arcs from endpoints of a base line, rather than trial-and-error with a ruler.
examples: Equilateral Triangle (4 cm): Arcs of 4 cm radius from points A and B meet at vertex C.
Scalene Construction (4, 5, 6 cm): Base AB (4 cm) with arcs of 5 cm from A and 6 cm from B.
Circle Centers Geometry: Using centers A and B of two equal-sized overlapping circles to form isosceles (∆ABC) and equilateral (∆ABD) triangles.

concepts.title: Circle Visualization for Triangle Existence
concepts.definition: The existence of a triangle depends on whether two circles (centered at base endpoints with radii equal to other sides) intersect. This relates to the Triangle Inequality.
formulae: Sum of two radii > Base length (leads to Case 3: internal intersection)
examples: Case 1: Circles touch (e.g., sides 3, 6, 9 where 3+6=9) → No triangle.
Case 2: Circles don't intersect (Sum < Base) → No triangle.
Equilateral Guarantee: An equilateral triangle of any sidelength (e.g., 50, 50, 50) is always possible because 50+50 > 50.

concepts.title: Construction Validity: SAS and ASA Constellations
concepts.definition: For a triangle to be constructed with angles, the sum of given angles must be $< 180^\circ$. If sum $= 180^\circ$, lines will be parallel and never meet.
examples: Triangle with 3 cm, 120°, 8 cm (SAS) is valid.
Triangle with 120°, 6 cm, 70° is impossible (Sum = 190 > 180).
If base angles are 40° and 140°, the sides are parallel and do not form a triangle.

concepts.title: Euclid's Proof: Angle Sum Property
concepts.definition: In 'The Elements' (300 BCE), Euclid proved the angle sum property by drawing a line through a vertex parallel to the opposite side.
examples: Deducing third angle (60) if two are 50 and 70.
Reflecting on the 'ingenious idea' of parallel lines to simplify geometric relationships.

concepts.title: Precise Altitude Construction with Geometry Tools
concepts.definition: An altitude is the height of a vertex with respect to the opposite side. Precise construction requires a set-square sliding along a ruler to achieve a true perpendicular (90°).
examples: Dropping altitude AD ⊥ BC by sliding a set-square till it touches vertex A.
Right-angled triangle: The sides containing the right angle are themselves the altitudes.
Obtuse triangle: The altitude from an acute vertex lies outside, requiring an extension of the base.

concepts.title: Classification Nuances: Acute, Right, and Obtuse
concepts.definition: Triangles are classified by angle measures. While a single right or obtuse angle defines those types, an acute-angled triangle must have ALL three angles acute.
examples: Acute-angled: All three angles < 90°.
Shortest path in a box riddle: Using spatial pathing on surfaces to reach the farthest corner.

concepts.title: Congruent Figures
concepts.definition: Two figures are congruent if they have exactly the same shape and size; they can be made to coincide by shifting, rotating or flipping.
examples: Two coins of the same denomination are congruent circles.

concepts.title: Congruence of Triangles
concepts.definition: Two triangles are congruent if all their corresponding sides and angles are equal in measure.
examples: If △ABC is placed exactly over △PQR and all vertices match, they are congruent.

concepts.title: Congruence Criteria: SSS, SAS, ASA, RHS
concepts.definition: Triangles are congruent if they satisfy any of these conditions: all three corresponding sides equal (SSS), two sides and included angle equal (SAS), two angles and included side equal (ASA) or right angle, hypotenuse and one side equal (RHS).
examples: If three sides of one triangle equal three sides of another, they are congruent by SSS.
Two right triangles with equal hypotenuse and one corresponding side are congruent by RHS.

concepts.title: The Correspondence Convention
concepts.definition: To express congruence accurately, vertices must be named in the exact order they overlap. ΔABC ≅ ΔXYZ means A corresponds to X, B to Y, and C to Z.
examples: Meera and Rabia's frame: Replicating a 40-60-80 cm triangular school frame by measuring corresponding sides.
If ΔHEN ≅ ΔBIG, then side HE = BI, EN = IG, and HN = BG.
In a rectangle ABCD, the diagonal BD divides it into ΔABD ≅ ΔCDB (note the reversed vertex order for correct overlap).

concepts.title: Non-Congruent Cases: AAA and SSA failure
concepts.definition: Not all three-part measurements guarantee congruence. AAA (Angle-Angle-Angle) ensures the same shape but different sizes. SSA (Side-Side-Angle) with a non-included angle can result in two distinct, non-congruent triangles.
examples: AAA Example: Triangles with 30°, 70°, and 80° can have varying side lengths.
SSA Example: Constructing a triangle with base PQ=6cm, ∠P=30°, and side QR=4cm results in two possible triangles ∆PQR and ∆PQS because the arc intersects the line at two points.

concepts.title: AAS (Angle-Angle-Side) Property
concepts.definition: Two triangles are congruent if any two pairs of angles and one pair of corresponding sides are equal. This is an extension of ASA using the Angle Sum Property.
examples: If ∠A = ∠X, ∠C = ∠Z and side BC = YZ, then ∠B must equal ∠Y, satisfying the ASA condition.

concepts.title: Congruence Proofs for Isosceles Properties
concepts.definition: Congruence can be used to deduce that angles opposite to equal sides are equal in an isosceles triangle.
formulae: If AB = AC, then ∠B = ∠C (Corresponding parts of congruent ∆ADB and ∆ADC)
examples: Drawing altitude AD from vertex A to base BC in isosceles ∆ABC shows the two halves are congruent by RHS.
Equilateral Proof: Since all sides are equal, using the isosceles logic repeatedly proves all angles = 180/3 = 60°.

concepts.title: Superposition and Line of Symmetry Logic
concepts.definition: Two figures are congruent if they can be exactly superimposed, potentially after rotation or flipping. Symmetry acts as an internal verification of congruence.
examples: Tracing a symbol on a signboard to replicate it exactly.
Observing diagonal symmetry in a square to prove ∆ABC ≅ ∆ADC.
Expression Engineer Puzzle: Splitting a region of white squares into 6 smaller congruent regions via division lines.

concepts.title: Congruence in Real-Life Architecture
concepts.definition: Congruent triangles provide structural stability and aesthetic symmetry in famous architectural landmarks.
examples: Louvre Museum (Paris): Symmetrical glass triangles in the pyramid structure.
Great Pyramid of Giza (Egypt): Massive congruent triangular faces.
Howrah Bridge (Kolkata): Congruent steel trusses for load distribution.
Rangoli and Dome designs using repeated congruent geometric twins.

concepts.title: Ratio and Proportion
concepts.definition: Ratio compares two quantities of the same kind; proportion states that two ratios are equal.
formulae: a : b = c : d ⇔ a/b = c/d
examples: The ratio of 2 to 5 is 2 : 5.

concepts.title: Percentage
concepts.definition: Percentage is a way of expressing a number as a fraction of 100.
examples: If a student scores 45 out of 50, percentage = (45/50) × 100 = 90%.

concepts.title: Profit and Loss
concepts.definition: Profit is the gain when selling price exceeds cost price; loss is the amount by which cost price exceeds selling price.
examples: If CP is ₹100 and SP is ₹120, profit = ₹20 and profit% = 20%.

concepts.title: Simple Interest
concepts.definition: Simple interest is interest calculated on the original principal only for the entire period.
examples: On ₹1000 at 5% per year for 2 years, SI = (1000 × 5 × 2) / 100 = ₹100.

concepts.title: Definition of Rational Numbers
concepts.definition: A rational number is any number that can be written in the form p/q where p and q are integers and q ≠ 0.
examples: 2/3, −5/7 and 4 (as 4/1) are rational numbers.

concepts.title: Equivalent Rational Numbers
concepts.definition: Rational numbers that represent the same point on the number line are equivalent; they can be obtained by multiplying or dividing numerator and denominator by the same non‑zero integer.
examples: 1/2, 2/4 and −3/−6 are equivalent rational numbers.

concepts.title: Standard Form of Rational Numbers
concepts.definition: A rational number is in standard form when it is written with a positive denominator and numerator and denominator have no common factor other than 1.
examples: −6/8 simplified to standard form is −3/4.

concepts.title: Operations on Rational Numbers
concepts.definition: Addition, subtraction, multiplication and division of rational numbers follow the same rules as for fractions, with sign rules applied.

concepts.title: Constructing a Line Parallel to a Given Line
concepts.definition: Using a ruler and compass or set‑square, a line can be drawn through a given point parallel to a given line.
examples: Through point P outside line l, draw line m parallel to l using a set‑square.

concepts.title: Constructing Triangles: SSS
concepts.definition: A triangle can be constructed when the lengths of all three sides are known, using a ruler and compass.
examples: Construct △ABC given AB, BC and CA by first drawing one side and then using two circles with given radii to locate the third vertex.

concepts.title: Constructing Triangles: SAS and ASA
concepts.definition: Triangles can be constructed when two sides and the included angle (SAS) or two angles and the included side (ASA) are known.
examples: To construct a triangle with sides AB and AC and included angle at A, draw AB, construct the angle at A and mark AC.

concepts.title: Constructing Right Triangles (RHS)
concepts.definition: Right‑angled triangles can be constructed when hypotenuse and one side are given, using a right angle at the appropriate vertex.
examples: Construct a right triangle with hypotenuse 5 cm and one side 3 cm using a right‑angle tool and compass.

concepts.title: Perpendicular Bisector and Midpoint Symmetry
concepts.definition: Bisection is the division of a geometrical object into two identical parts. A perpendicular bisector of a line segment passes through its midpoint at a 90° angle.
formulae: AX = AY = BX = BY (Condition for perpendicular bisector AB of XY)
examples: The 'Eyes' construction: Drawing symmetrical upper and lower arcs using points A and B on the perpendicular bisector.
Proving bisection accuracy by showing ∆AOX ≅ ∆AOY via SAS congruence.

concepts.title: Historical Sulba-Sutra Rope Methods
concepts.definition: Ancient Indian geometric texts (Vedic period) describing exact procedures for constructing perpendiculars and altars using ropes and pegs.
examples: Katyāyana-ulbastra 1.2: Using a looped rope stretched from two poles (X and Y) to find the bisector points A and B.
Ropes as flexible geometric tools: Capable of forming straight lines (stretched) or arcs (fixed radius).

concepts.title: Angle Bisection and Angle Copying
concepts.definition: Any angle can be bisected or exactly replicated without measurement using only a ruler and compass, relying on SSS congruence properties.
examples: Creating an 8-petalled lotus(Fig. 6.5) design by repeatedly bisecting 360° into 45° segments.
Copying an unknown angle ∠A to ∠X by constructing congruent isosceles triangles ∆ABC ≅ ∆XYZ.

concepts.title: Structural Arch Design with Support Lines
concepts.definition: Architectural arches (like Trefoil or Pointed arches) are constructed using symmetrical support line segments and specific arc radii for stability and aesthetics.
examples: Trefoil Arch (Diwan-i-Aam, Red Fort): Requiring support lines AB=CD and equal base angles.
Pointed Gothic Arch: Constructed by marking midpoints on two supporting line segments and drawing intersecting arcs.
Inflexed Arc: A complex symmetrical curve constructed using only a compass.
Wavy Wave (Grade 6 revisit): Using two equal line segments as supporting lines and marking their midpoints to direct the arc sequence.

concepts.title: Regular Hexagon and 60° Construction
concepts.definition: A regular hexagon can be decomposed into six congruent equilateral triangles meeting at a central point. Each interior angle is 120°.
examples: Constructing a 60° angle by drawing an equilateral triangle on a line segment.
6-Pointed Star construction: Layering equilateral triangles (∆AGH, etc.) on a hexagonal base.

concepts.title: Tiling Theory and Grid Parity
concepts.definition: Tiling (tessellation) is covering a region with shapes without gaps or overlaps. Grid tileability depends on the parity of rows and columns.
formulae: m × n grid is tileable with 2×1 tiles if either m or n is even.
Odd × Odd grid is never tileable (total squares is odd).
examples: Black-and-White Grid Proof: A $5 \times 3$ grid with one square removed is non-tileable if the number of remaining black and white squares is unequal.
Tangram Puzzles: Reconstructing an arrow or bird shape using 7 fixed geometric pieces.

concepts.title: Perfect Tessellation of the Plane
concepts.definition: The infinite plane can be tiled using regular polygons like squares, equilateral triangles, and hexagons because their interior angles divide 360° perfectly.
examples: Beehive cells: Natural hexagonal tiling that minimizes wasted space and material.
Escher-style Tiling: Creative tessellations using animal shapes and complex periodic patterns.
Optical Illusion: Creating visual depth and movement patterns using repeated geometric tessellations.
2023 Discovery: Periodic tiling using more than one non-regular polygon shape.

concepts.title: Perimeter
concepts.definition: Perimeter is the total length of the boundary of a closed figure.
examples: A rectangle 5 cm by 3 cm has perimeter 2 × (5 + 3) = 16 cm.

concepts.title: Area of Rectangle and Square
concepts.definition: Area is the region enclosed by a closed figure. For rectangles and squares it equals the product of side lengths.
examples: A square of side 4 cm has area 16 cm².

concepts.title: Area of Triangle and Parallelogram (Introductory)
concepts.definition: Area of a triangle is half the product of its base and height; area of a parallelogram is product of base and corresponding height.
examples: A triangle with base 6 cm and height 4 cm has area (1/2) × 6 × 4 = 12 cm².

concepts.title: Terms, Coefficients and Factors
concepts.definition: An algebraic expression is made of terms; each term has a numeric coefficient and one or more variable factors.
examples: In 5xy, 5 is the coefficient and x, y are variable factors.
In 7a − 3, 7a and −3 are terms.

concepts.title: Like and Unlike Terms
concepts.definition: Like terms have exactly the same variable part (including powers); unlike terms differ in variable part or exponents.
examples: 3x and −5x are like terms; 2x and 2y are unlike terms.
4a²b and −7a²b are like terms.

concepts.title: Addition and Subtraction of Expressions
concepts.definition: Algebraic expressions are added or subtracted by combining like terms.
examples: In (x + y) + 5, grouping (x + y) first does not change the total value.
Using the distributive property: a(b + c) = ab + ac.

concepts.title: Multiplication of a Monomial by a Monomial
concepts.definition: Multiplying two monomials multiplies their coefficients and adds exponents of like variables.

concepts.title: Multiplication of a Monomial by a Polynomial
concepts.definition: To multiply a monomial by a polynomial, multiply the monomial with each term of the polynomial and add results.

concepts.title: The Notion of Letter-Numbers & Contextual Variables
concepts.definition: Letter-numbers (variables) are letters used as concise symbols to represent unknown or changing values in mathematical relations.
formulae: s = a + 3 (Shabnam's age is 3 more than Aftab's)
examples: If Aftab's age 'a' is 23, Shabnam's age 's' is 23 + 3 = 26.
Cost of c coconuts and j kgs of jaggery: 35c + 60j.

concepts.title: General Formulas & Rule of Omission
concepts.definition: Algebraic expressions allow for concise general formulas. A standard practice is to omit the multiplication symbol between numbers and letters (e.g., 4 × n becomes 4n).
examples: Munirathna's pipe: Connecting a 20m pipe to another of length 'k' results in 20 + k.
Time taken to grind y kg of grain in a mill: 10 + 8y (10s startup + 8s per kg).
Yahapur to Vahapur train time: 4t + 6 minutes (4 segments of length t and 3 stops of 2 mins each).
Snail in a well: Total progress after 10 days and nights: 10(u - d) cm, but if d > u, it slips down more than it climbs.

concepts.title: Simplification & Grid Sum Invariants
concepts.definition: Expressions are simplified by grouping like terms. Simplified forms (e.g., 2l + 2b) are equal to the initial form (l + b + l + b) as they yield identical values for any input.
examples: Calendar Property: In any 2x2 square, the sum of diagonal numbers (a and a+8) equals the sum of (a+1 and a+7).
Distributive simplification: 5c + 3c + 10c = (5+3+10)c = 18c.

concepts.title: Number Machines & Sequence Mapping
concepts.definition: Algebraic expressions act as 'Number Machines' that perform fixed operations on inputs to produce consistent output patterns.
formulae: Rule: 2a - b (Two times the first number minus the second)
nth term of Saree Design C = 3n
examples: Design A, B, and C positions in a repeating saree border: Design A occurs at 3n-2, B at 3n-1, and C at 3n.
Formula Detective: Input (5, 2) → Output (8) follow the 2a - b rule.

concepts.title: Matchstick Logic & Step Formulas
concepts.definition: Stepwise growth in matchstick patterns can be described by general linear expressions based on the rate of increase per step.
examples: Step 33 of a triangle pattern requires 2(33) + 1 = 67 matchsticks.
Proving equivalence: 3 + 2(y-1) simplifies to 2y + 1 using the distributive property.

concepts.title: Cyclic Patterns & Grid Coordinate Systems
concepts.definition: Algebra can map positions in cyclic sequences (traffic signals) or infinite grids using row and column coordinates.
formulae: Grid Value = 4(r - 1) + c (For a 4-column endless grid)
examples: Determining position 122 in a 4-column grid: Divide 122 by 4 to find it falls in Row 31, Column 2.
Traffic color at position 343: Since 343 matches the 4n-1 pattern (remainder 3), it is Green.

concepts.title: Exponents and Expanded Form
concepts.definition: Exponents are a shorthand notation for repeated multiplication of the same number.

concepts.title: Laws of Exponents (Same Base)
concepts.definition: Exponents with the same base follow specific rules for multiplication and division.

concepts.title: Power of a Power and Product
concepts.definition: Raising a power to another power multiplies exponents; raising a product to a power raises each factor.

concepts.title: Negative and Zero Exponents (Introductory)
concepts.definition: Negative exponents represent reciprocals and zero exponent represents 1 for any non‑zero base.

concepts.title: Standard Form (Scientific Notation)
concepts.definition: Very large or very small numbers can be written in standard form as a × 10^n, where 1 ≤ a < 10 and n is an integer.

concepts.title: Line Symmetry
concepts.definition: A figure has line symmetry if it can be folded along a line so that both halves coincide exactly.
examples: An equilateral triangle has 3 lines of symmetry; a square has 4.

concepts.title: Lines of Symmetry in Standard Figures
concepts.definition: Regular figures often have multiple lines of symmetry depending on the number of equal sides and angles.
examples: A regular pentagon has 5 lines of symmetry.
A circle has infinitely many lines of symmetry.

concepts.title: Relation with Reflections
concepts.definition: Reflection in a mirror produces a symmetric image across the mirror line; the mirror line is a line of symmetry.
examples: Letters like A, H and O show line symmetry when reflected across certain lines.

concepts.title: 2D and 3D Shapes
concepts.definition: 2D shapes have length and breadth (like squares, circles); 3D shapes have length, breadth and height (like cubes, spheres).
examples: A rectangle is 2D; a cuboid like a brick is 3D.

concepts.title: Faces, Edges and Vertices
concepts.definition: Faces are flat surfaces of a solid, edges are line segments where faces meet, and vertices are points where edges meet.
examples: A cube has 6 faces, 12 edges and 8 vertices.

concepts.title: Nets of 3D Shapes
concepts.definition: A net is a 2D pattern that can be folded to form a 3D solid.
examples: A cross‑shaped arrangement of 6 equal squares can be folded into a cube.

concepts.title: Different Views of 3D Objects
concepts.definition: A 3D object can look different from front, side and top; these projections help visualise and draw solids.
examples: A cylinder looks like a rectangle from the side and a circle from the top.
